peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and very high extraversion, combined with strong civic engagement and a history of special education support, yield clear support for empowering citizens through literacy, moderated by low digital literacy and low trust in mainstream institutions.
Very high agreeableness and altruism, combined with low digital literacy and a desire to protect others, strongly favor educational interventions.

High openness (73.07) and very high conscientiousness (78.51) combine with strong moral fairness/equality concerns to produce clear support for educational solutions, while populist people-centrism (94.32) aligns with empowering citizens.

High internal political efficacy and advanced digital literacy, combined with a strong global identity, drive solid support for empowering citizens through education, though very low conscientiousness slightly reduces the intensity.
High openness to experience values and belief in political trust make education an appealing structural fix, though low digital literacy and limited personal engagement keep support moderate.
Despite moderate agreement on principle, her own low digital literacy (low), low openness (26.35), and home-schooling background reduce enthusiasm for formalized state-led literacy programs.

Very high agreeableness, high openness, and strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference align with robust support for educational interventions, while very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence prevent maximal endorsement.

Low openness to experience (32.78) and very low conscientiousness (15.0) reduce enthusiasm for educational interventions, while high populist anti-elitism (74.42) and distrust of institutions (23.25) make this feel like elite-driven indoctrination, yielding mild opposition.

Very high authority deference and trust in existing institutions (97.75) reduce need for new regulation, while populist anti-elitism (83.76) sees platforms as elite accomplices, yielding mild opposition overall.

High trust in institutions is offset by very low agreeableness, strong anti-egalitarianism, and a preference for freedom from moderation, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence about holding platforms accountable.
Moderate trust in government is undermined by very low agreeableness and anti-censorship orientation, leading to a balanced but slightly negative view of regulation.

Extreme anti-censorship (98.59) and anti-domination (96.97) create strong resistance to regulation, but her moderate system distrust (low system justification 2.79) and occasional civic engagement (44.26) leave the score near neutral.
Strong authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control and high trust in government support regulation, but very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence moderate the score.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (71.8) and anti-censorship (73.39) oppose regulation, but authoritarian punitive attitudes (79.87) and low agreeableness (29.48) create some desire to hold platforms accountable, resulting in mild opposition.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate trust in government and high political efficacy, combined with low authoritarian submission and high openness, produce mild support for public broadcasting as a source of reliable information, though low trust in mainstream journalism tempers it.
High trust in institutions and experts, combined with high loyalty and patriotism, strongly favor funding public broadcasting as a trusted source.

Extremely high traditional-authoritarian nationalist values (99.94) combined with high national identification (86.64) and group-based dominance (47.92) strongly endorse state-funded broadcasting that upholds national culture and unity.

High trust in government and science, combined with a cosmopolitan orientation and strong civic engagement, produces solid support for public broadcasting as a trusted information source, despite low conscientiousness.
Moderate institutional trust and collectivist values favor funding, but libertarian skepticism of state media and low social trust make support only mild.

High trust in science (57.81) and moderate trust in government (51.62) offer some support, but her general anti-state-control libertarianism (94.11) and low cultural openness (69.68) pull it slightly negative.

Very high agreeableness, strong economic left-wing welfare state support, and high trust in institutions align with robust backing for public broadcasting, though low openness to actions slightly limits enthusiasm.

Very low trust in government (15.19) and mainstream institutions (23.25), combined with high anti-elitism (74.42) and a competitive-jungle worldview (94.76), produce clear opposition to funding what is seen as establishment propaganda.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, but low digital literacy limits the strength of this stance.
Libertarian preference for choice supports mandates, but low digital literacy and low conscientiousness lead to ambivalence about implementation.

High openness and individualism from low neuroticism facets support user choice, but strong deference to authority weakens enthusiasm for mandated options; moderate support emerges from fairness and liberty values.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high digital confidence support user choice, but very low agreeableness and high social dominance orientation make this a mild rather than strong preference.
Openness to diverse information sources and fairness concerns drive mild support, but her very low extraversion and digital deprivation limit enthusiasm.

Her strong pro-liberty moral foundation (96.97 anti-domination) and moderate internal political efficacy (80.89) align with giving users control over algorithms, though her own low digital confidence (low) makes her unsure of the mechanism.
High openness and GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference support user choice, but very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence yield only mild, lukewarm support.

Libertarian anti-censorship (73.39) and privacy orientation (61.69) support user choice, while low openness (32.78) and high authoritarianism (82.83) temper enthusiasm, resulting in moderate support for mandated choice.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and high internal political efficacy, combined with strong anti-domination values and moderate trust in institutions, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media as a transparency measure, though low trust in others tempers it.
High trust in experts and concern for harm (care foundation) support labeling, while libertarian anti-censorship orientation slightly reduces intensity.
High conscientiousness (78.51) and sanctity/degradation concerns (74.14) push for labeling to maintain trust, while low anti-censorship (10.5) limits opposition, yielding moderate clear support.
High trust in science and experts, plus advanced digital literacy, drive support for labeling synthetic media, while very low agreeableness and a competitive worldview keep this at a moderate level.
Protective of truth and fairness, high institutional trust supports mandatory labeling, though mild libertarian impulses and low agreeableness keep it from being very strong.

Her high anti-manipulation stance (populist manichean worldview 60.93) and concern about foreign information manipulation (see other leaves) support labels, while her libertarian anti-censorship (98.59) limits strong endorsement.

High trust in science and experts, combined with authoritarian law-and-order preferences, drives clear support for labelling, though low libertarian anti-censorship orientation prevents maximal endorsement.

High dangerous-worldview (84.1) and authoritarian law-and-order (81.76) drive strong support for labeling to prevent deception, though libertarian anti-censorship (73.39) slightly reduces the score from maximal.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low agreeableness and low trust in others, combined with very high openness and low authoritarian submission, produce clear opposition to trusting mainstream journalism, as the person sees it as a tool of elite control.
Very high trust in institutions and experts, combined with high agreeableness and low authoritarianism, strongly favor trust in mainstream journalism.

Exceptionally high trust in all institutions (97.75) and system justification (73.96) combined with low dangerous-worldview (0.47) produce strong trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate pillar of national order.

Despite high trust in government and institutions, very low agreeableness and high anti-egalitarianism, combined with a populist anti-elitist streak, produce mild distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite institution.
Despite high institutional trust, very low agreeableness and strong populist anti-elitism produce a mildly negative personal stance toward mainstream journalism.

High populist anti-elitism (79.08) and anti-system justification (1.77 political system justification) produce skepticism of mainstream journalism, yet moderate trust in institutions (52.88) prevents complete distrust.

High trust in institutions and very high agreeableness support moderate trust, but low news-seeking behavior and high collective narcissism about ingroup narratives temper confidence in mainstream outlets.

Extremely low trust in institutions (23.25), high anti-elitism (74.42), and a Manichean worldview (90.41) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism, consistent with the profile's populist and nationalist orientation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong anti-domination values and very high internal political efficacy, combined with low agreeableness and high openness, produce strong support for protecting researchers who expose platform misconduct, as a check on corporate power.
High agreeableness and fairness concerns support protections, but low authoritarianism and high trust in institutions reduce perceived need.

Very high authoritarian submission (4.11) and low libertarianism (7.13) oppose protections that threaten institutional authority, while strong group-based dominance (47.92) sees researcher whistleblowing as disloyal, producing clear opposition.

High trust in science and experts, combined with a strong sense of civic engagement and very low agreeableness, supports protecting researchers who expose wrongdoing, though high social dominance orientation slightly dampens enthusiasm.
Strong fairness, anti-domination, and openness to outsider voices combine with intact institutional trust to clearly support protections for whistleblowers.

Very high anti-authoritarian and pro-transparency values (94.11 state skepticism, 98.59 anti-censorship) strongly support protecting whistleblowers from retaliatory silencing, consistent with her anti-domination moral foundation (96.97).

High trust in science and GAL-TAN civil liberties orientation support protections, but very low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission produce only mild, conditional support.

High libertarian civil liberties support (69.92) and anti-elitism (74.42) favor protections, but low trust in experts (15.1) and high collective narcissism (88.52) create ambivalence, resulting in mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in government and high system justification, combined with low dangerous-worldview and low authoritarian submission, produce mild opposition to distrusting elections, as the person generally trusts institutional processes.
Extremely high trust in government and institutions, combined with high agreeableness and low authoritarianism, leads to strong rejection of election distrust.

Very high trust in government (94.6) and the legislature (97.84), combined with strong system justification (73.96), produce clear opposition to election distrust, despite populist people-centrism (94.32) that theoretically challenges elite control.
